html:
Alva Myrdal, born Reimer on January 31, 1902, in Uppsala, and died on February 1, 1986, in Danderyd, was a Swedish social democratic politician and diplomat. She served as a government minister from 1966 to 1973 and received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1982 (shared with Alfonso García Robles of Mexico).

== Biography ==

=== Childhood and Education === Alva Myrdal grew up as the eldest child in a relatively well-off family, the daughter of construction contractor Albert Reimer (1876–1943) and Lowa Jonsson (1877–1943). She had four siblings: Rut (1904–1959), Folke (1906–1977), Maj (1909–1941), and Stig (1912–1977). Her father was a socialist, and her mother was a liberal. The family also subscribed to two daily newspapers. During her childhood, the family moved to various locations, including Eskilstuna, Älvsjö, and Stockholm. Her academic studies included psychology and family sociology. She earned a Bachelor of Arts degree in Stockholm in 1924. In 1929, Alva and her husband Gunnar Myrdal were given the opportunity to travel to the United States as Rockefeller fellows. For a year, Alva had the chance to further deepen her studies in psychology, pedagogy, and sociology. She particularly had the opportunity to expand her knowledge of child development. Observing the significant social and economic disparities in the United States also led to increased political engagement – "radical" became the term she and Gunnar used to describe their shared political views. They then moved to Geneva for further studies, where they also studied the population decline that concerned many Europeans during the interwar period.

=== Family Policy and Population Issues ===

Alva and Gunnar Myrdal's book, "Crisis in the Population Question," published in 1934, is one of the most discussed Swedish political writings of the 20th century. In this book, they argued for the importance of sharing responsibility for raising children between parents and society through trained childcare educators.

Alva was strongly critical of the development of preschool programs in Sweden. Therefore, she published the book "City Children" (1935), where she presented ideas for a new, reformed Swedish preschool system based on modern child psychology. She believed that existing childcare options – nurseries and kindergartens – were both inadequate. Nurseries were essentially a form of welfare assistance, while kindergartens were an expensive school-like program designed to prepare children for private schools. She emphasized that material circumstances often hindered good upbringing, and therefore social and economic reforms were necessary. Alva wanted to combine nurseries and kindergartens into a "large childcare center," ideally located in an HSB housing complex (designed for city children). At this time, there was a debate between those who advocated for an "authoritarian" approach to child-rearing and those who preferred a "free" approach. She shared the criticism of the authoritarian view but argued that proponents of free upbringing had no viable alternative. In Alva's solution, "collaboration" between the caregiver and the child became a key concept.

A year later, she had the opportunity to move from theory to practice. Alva became the director of the Social Pedagogical Seminary, which she helped found in 1936. There, she worked as a teacher, training preschool teachers. Alva believed that previous preschool teacher education had not sufficiently incorporated the latest pedagogical research. Therefore, her program aimed to integrate new findings in child psychology into the curriculum. Civic education was also emphasized, along with the personal development of women.

She was a member of the Committee for Increased Representation of Women, which was formed in 1937 to increase the representation of women in politics.

Alva Myrdal actively participated in the planning of the house, Villa Myrdal, which was built on two floors at Nyängsvägen 155 in Stora Mossen, Bromma. The architect Sven Markelius, who was a close friend of Alva and Gunnar Myrdal, designed the house in 1937.

In 1938, Alva and Gunnar Myrdal traveled to the United States. While in the US, Alva published the book "Nation and Family" (1941), which discussed Swedish family and population policy. Myrdal was a member of the board for Sweden's participation in the New York World's Fair in 1939. During World War II, she also lived in Sweden on several occasions. While in Stockholm, she attended meetings of the anti-Nazi organization "Tuesday Club."

=== The Post-World War II Era – A Flourishing Career ===

After the war, Alva participated in several social policy investigations. She was a member of the 1940 School Commission, which later resulted in a proposal for a nine-year compulsory primary school system, which is the current system. During the post-war period, Alva Myrdal became increasingly involved in international affairs. From 1949 to 1950, she was the head of the United Nations Department of Social Affairs. From 1951 to 1955, she headed UNESCO's social sciences department – the first woman to hold such high positions within the UN. Subsequently, she became Sweden's ambassador to India from 1956 to 1961. After that, she was elected as a member of parliament from 1962 to 1970, representing the Social Democratic Labour Party (the first chamber). Following Ulla Lindst...

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the 20th century is extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts but rather interpretation and evaluation, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: Alva Myrdal was a prominent Swedish politician and diplomat who dedicated much of her work to children's rights and education. She was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1982 for her efforts.
lattlast:
Alva Myrdal, born in 1902, became a prominent figure in politics and social issues. She studied topics such as child development and family dynamics. Together with her husband, she wrote books that influenced how Sweden cared for its children and implemented its population policies.

She dedicated much of her work to improving preschool education and believed that children should have a good start in life through effective teaching methods (pedagogy). She was also the first woman to hold high-level positions within the United Nations and UNESCO, which are major international organizations. Later in her career, she served as an ambassador to India and was a member of parliament.

Alva Myrdal gained great respect for her work promoting peace and human rights. In 1982, she received the Nobel Peace Prize, one of the world's highest honors awarded to individuals working towards global peace.
